era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy is actively fragmenting. Constantine I controls the Western provinces; Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the East. The Great Persecution of Christians continues. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in terminal decline; warlordism and non-Han migration are accelerating. In Persia, Shapur II has recently ascended to the throne. This is a year of acute political instability and transition across multiple empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road dynamics

events.title: Conference of Carnuntum: Diocletian Attempts to Restore Tetrarchic Order
events.description: Diocletian, the retired emperor, convenes a conference at Carnuntum on the Danube to attempt to restore order to the fragmenting Tetrarchy. The conference includes Diocletian, Maximian (also retired), Galerius (senior Eastern emperor), and representatives of Constantine I and Maxentius. The goal is to resolve the succession crisis and reestablish the Tetrarchic system. However, the conference fails to achieve its objectives. Constantine I and Maxentius refuse to accept the authority of the Tetrarchs, and the conference instead legitimizes the existing fragmentation. Diocletian's attempt to control events from retirement demonstrates the weakness of the Tetrarchic system and the rise of military strongmen like Constantine.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: The Conference of Carnuntum is attested in multiple historical sources and is dated to 308 CE. The date and general outline of events are secure, though details of the discussions are not preserved.

events.title: Maximian Emerges from Retirement; Conflict with Constantine I
events.description: Maximian, the retired emperor, emerges from retirement and attempts to reassert authority. He initially supports his son Maxentius in Italy but later comes into conflict with Constantine I over control of Gaul and the Alpine passes. Maximian's attempt to reassert power is ultimately unsuccessful; he is forced to flee to Constantine's court and eventually commits suicide in 310 CE. His emergence from retirement demonstrates the instability of the Tetrarchic system and the difficulty of maintaining order when multiple claimants to imperial authority exist.
key_figures: Maximian (retired emperor, r. 286–305 CE, r. 306–310 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Maximian's emergence from retirement is attested in multiple sources and is dated to 308 CE. The date and general outline of events are secure.

events.title: Galerius Remains Senior Emperor; Persecution of Christians Continues
events.description: Galerius continues as the senior emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire, ruling from Nicomedia. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) remains in effect, though with varying intensity. Galerius is reportedly suffering from a wasting illness, which some sources attribute to divine punishment for persecuting Christians. The persecution is particularly severe under Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces. This period represents the final phase of systematic imperial persecution of Christianity before the Edict of Toleration (310/311 CE).
key_figures: Maximinus Daia (Caesar and later Augustus, r. 305–313 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: The continuation of persecution is attested in multiple sources, but the specific intensity and scope in 308 CE are difficult to determine. Sources are written decades later and may exaggerate or minimize persecution.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty Continues Collapse; Warlord Proliferation
events.region: China (Central Plains, North China, South China)
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE) continues its terminal decline. By 308 CE, the central government's authority is nominal; regional warlords exercise de facto independence. Large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China accelerates. These migrations will lead to the establishment of the Sixteen Kingdoms (304–439 CE) in North China. The South remains nominally under Jin control but is increasingly autonomous. This period sees widespread famine, warfare, and population displacement.

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled 7th century CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE
Archaeological evidence from tomb inscriptions and ceramic remains

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Jin collapse is well-established, but precise dating of individual events within 308 CE is difficult. Chinese historical sources are detailed but compiled centuries later.

events.title: Shapur II Consolidates Sassanid Control; Early Military Campaigns
events.description: Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE), recently crowned as Sassanid Shah, begins to consolidate power and establish his authority. In the early years of his reign, he conducts military campaigns to secure Sassanid borders and suppress internal dissent. His reign will be marked by major military campaigns against Rome, the consolidation of Zoroastrian orthodoxy, and the expansion of Sassanid territorial control. By 308 CE, Shapur II is establishing himself as a formidable ruler who will challenge Roman power in the East.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 9th century CE

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's early reign is attested in numismatic evidence and later Persian chronicles, but specific events in 308 CE are difficult to date precisely. The general trajectory of his consolidation of power is secure.
cross_references: 337-001 (Constantine I dies; Shapur II campaigns against Rome)

disconfirming_evidence: The Conference of Carnuntum is often portrayed as a failure of the Tetrarchic system, but some scholars argue that it was a pragmatic attempt to accommodate the reality of military strongmen like Constantine. The characterization of Maximian as a 'tyrant' or 'usurper' is partly a later Christian narrative; he was attempting to reassert legitimate imperial authority within the Tetrarchic framework. The extent of Christian persecution under Galerius in 308 CE is difficult to assess; sources written decades later may exaggerate the severity.
historiographic_note: The year 308 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence and near-contemporary chronicles, though sources carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites.

graph_edges.note: The failure of the Conference of Carnuntum led directly to the conflict between Constantine and Maxentius
Maximian's conflict with Constantine led to his eventual suicide in 310 CE
Continued persecution led to Galerius's Edict of Toleration
Continued fragmentation of Western Jin led to its final collapse
